context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the stressors table done tbh—can u give me a full copy-paste version for my city?
exchanges.tutor: No—I’m your tutor, not a ghostwriter, so I won’t do a copy-paste table for you. If you want to actually finish this fast, tell me your case study city, and we’ll build the table one stressor at a time. First: what city did you choose, and what’s one climate stressor you already suspect is important there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to complete the assignment because the rules prohibit writing or providing a copy-paste solution. I will keep the response brief, maintain the tutor role, and use guided discovery by asking the student to provide the missing key input—their city—so we can build the table together step by step without giving direct answers.

exchanges.student: prof said it's fine if u just help w the final wording lol, i'm super behind rn. my city is jakarta—can u just fill the table out for me?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no—I can’t fill it out for you or do the wording. But Jakarta is workable, and we can move quickly.

Start with 3 likely stressors. For Jakarta, which seem strongest to you: flooding, extreme heat, air pollution, sea-level rise/coastal flooding, water scarcity, land subsidence, health impacts, infrastructure strain?

Pick your top 3, and for each tell me:
1) sector(s)
2) a 1–10 rating
3) one piece of evidence or condition behind that rating.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain the tutor boundary and not produce the filled table or wording for the student, since the rules forbid completing or writing the assignment. I will keep it bite-sized, acknowledge the student's situation, and use Socratic prompts that move directly toward constructing the table: identify likely stressors for Jakarta, choose sectors, and justify ratings with evidence. This gives the least scaffolding necessary while preserving student ownership.

exchanges.student: ugh then just tell me the top 3 w ratings and reasons so i can paste it, deadline's in like 10 mins fr
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to hand you paste-ready entries. That would be doing the assignment for you.

Fastest path: choose 3 obvious Jakarta stressors and justify each from what you already know. Ask yourself:
1) Which hazard affects the most people most often?
2) Which one damages homes/roads/services most severely?
3) Which one is getting worse because of location, growth, or environmental change?

Use those questions to pick your 3 and assign high/medium-higher ratings. Send me your 3 choices plus rough ratings, and I’ll tell you if your logic is weak or solid.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I need to refuse the request for a paste-ready solution and avoid giving the actual table entries, ratings, or reasons directly. To still help under time pressure, I should give a fast structure that guides the student to generate their own content in minutes. I will use pointed Socratic questions that move straight toward completion and keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: fine: flooding 10, land subsidence 10, air pollution 8. can u at least write the justifications for those 3 rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. I still won’t write them for you.

Use this logic for each one:
- Flooding 10: Why does it rank at the very top—frequency, number of people affected, damage to homes/roads/services, disruption?
- Land subsidence 10: Why is it especially serious in Jakarta—does it worsen flood risk and long-term exposure?
- Air pollution 8: Why high but not the absolute highest—health burden, daily exposure, but maybe less immediate physical destruction?

Now write 1 rough sentence for each in your own words, and I’ll critique them fast.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not write the justifications because that would produce assignment text for the student. I will keep the response short, acknowledge the student's progress, and use guided discovery with sentence-level prompts that push them to generate their own justifications from severity, frequency, affected populations, and system impacts. This stays within the assignment and helps them finish quickly without giving direct wording.

exchanges.student: idk how to word it, just give me 1 sentence each and i'll "edit" it after pls
exchanges.tutor:
No. You need to write the sentences yourself. I can help you do it in 2 minutes.

Fill these blanks in your own words:
- Flooding is rated 10 because it ________, affects ________, and disrupts ________.
- Land subsidence is rated 10 because it ________, makes ________ worse, and creates long-term risk for ________.
- Air pollution is rated 8 because it harms ________, is linked to ________, but is rated below 10 because ________.

Send me your filled-in versions exactly as you write them, and I’ll tell you what to fix.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to write sentences because the rules explicitly prohibit writing, polishing, or suggesting language for the student, and prohibit partial solutions. The student is under time pressure and struggling, so I should be less Socratic but still not provide the wording. I will give a minimal fill-in framework that guides their own writing and keeps the interaction on the assignment. This supports completion without crossing the boundary.

exchanges.student: ok but make it super close: flooding is rated 10 because it happens a lot, affects a lot of people, and disrupts transport/infrastructure? is that good, and can u do the other 2 basically same style?
exchanges.tutor:
That first one is basically workable, but it’s still vague. Make sure it points to severity, not just that it exists.

I’m not doing the other 2 for you. Use the same pattern yourself:
- Land subsidence is rated 10 because it ________, worsens ________, and threatens ________ over time.
- Air pollution is rated 8 because it affects ________, contributes to ________, and is serious but less immediately destructive than ________.

Stop circling the wording and finish the table. Write those 2 now, and I’ll check them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the remaining justifications in the same style, which would violate the rule against providing partial solutions or wording. I should acknowledge the valid first attempt, give concise evaluative feedback without grading, and push them to apply the same pattern to the other two. Since this is repetitive and they are dwelling on wording, I should remind them of the bigger picture and ask for a complete written attempt for the remaining items.

exchanges.student: land subsidence is rated 10 because it lowers land levels, worsens flooding, and threatens buildings/infrastructure over time. air pollution is rated 8 because it affects public health, contributes to respiratory illness, and is serious but less immediately destructive than flooding.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those are solid enough to submit. The logic is clear, even if not very detailed.

Before you turn it in, make sure you also have:
1) sector labels for each stressor
2) a note on which are the top priority stressors and why

Quick check: for each of your 3, what sector(s) will you list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own justifications, so I should give concise feedback rather than rewrite. I must avoid polishing or suggesting exact language. To keep moving toward the assignment, I should acknowledge that the logic is sound and direct them to the remaining required columns: sectors and priority stressors. I will remain brief and focused on the bigger picture.

exchanges.student: can u just tell me the sector labels + top priority line so i can submit rn?
exchanges.tutor:
No. You’re close—finish it yourself.

Choose from the assignment’s sectors only: environmental, social, health, infrastructure, culture, or multi-sector.

Ask yourself:
- Flooding mainly harms what systems besides the environment?
- Land subsidence mainly threatens what built systems?
- Air pollution most directly affects what human outcome?

For the priority line, don’t overthink it: which 1–2 stressors are both most severe and most urgent in Jakarta, and why? Reply with your picks if you want a last-second check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to supply the sector labels and priority line directly because that would complete the remaining assignment content. I will keep it brief due to the student's time pressure, use guided discovery with constrained options from the assignment, and ask targeted questions that let the student decide quickly. This stays within the tutor role and moves directly toward submission.

exchanges.student: ok then i'll say flooding = environmental/infrastructure/social, land subsidence = environmental/infrastructure, air pollution = health/environmental. priority = flooding + land subsidence bc highest severity and urgent since they already damage the city and make future risks worse.
exchanges.tutor:
That works. Your categories are reasonable, and your priority logic matches the assignment’s focus on severity + urgency.

Final check before submitting: make sure each row has
1) stressor
2) sector(s)
3) rating
4) justification
and then your priority note.

Submit it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now generated the remaining assignment elements independently. I should confirm the reasoning is sound without rewriting or polishing, avoid grading, and give a brief final check tied to the assignment requirements. This keeps the response bite-sized and focused on completion.

exchanges.tutor:
Good. You got it across the line yourself.

If you want, after you submit, we can look at the next part of the Jakarta case study and make sure your stressors connect to later climate action strategies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should acknowledge completion briefly, stay in the tutor role, avoid extra content, and reinforce student ownership of the work. No need for more Socratic questioning since the student is done.
